text: There is something quietly beautiful about the way light passes through a glass of water on a sunny windowsill. The glass itself is nothing special, maybe a plain drinking glass, slightly scratched from years of use. But when the sun hits it at the right angle, it throws little rippling arcs of light onto the table or wall nearby, trembling slightly whenever someone walks past or a truck rumbles down the street outside. The light bends and scatters in ways that feel almost alive. It costs nothing. It asks for nothing. It happens whether anyone is watching or not. And for a moment, a completely ordinary Tuesday morning kitchen becomes a place where something lovely is occurring, unannounced and unrepeated, because the angle of the sun will shift within minutes and it will be gone.
